epigraph.text: Divide your portion to seven, or even to eight, for you know not what disaster may happen on earth.

content.text:
When a line passes through a triangle parallel to one of its sides, 
something remarkable happens: it creates proportional segments on 
the other two sides. This fundamental property, known since ancient 
times, has profound applications in architecture, surveying, and 
countless geometric proofs.

content.title: Triangle Proportionality Theorem (Side-Splitter)
content.content:
If a line is parallel to one side of a triangle and intersects the 
other two sides, then it divides those sides proportionally.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Since $\overline{DE} \parallel \overline{BC}$:

- $\angle ADE \cong \angle ABC$ (corresponding angles)

- $\angle AED \cong \angle ACB$ (corresponding angles)

- $\angle A \cong \angle A$ (reflexive property)

By AA Similarity, $\triangle ADE \sim \triangle ABC$.

From the similarity, corresponding sides are proportional:
\[
\frac{AD}{AB} = \frac{AE}{AC}
\]

Since $AB = AD + DB$ and $AC = AE + EC$:
\[
\frac{AD}{AD + DB} = \frac{AE}{AE + EC}
\]

Using the property that if $\frac{a}{b} = \frac{c}{d}$, then 
$\frac{a}{b-a} = \frac{c}{d-c}$:
\[
\frac{AD}{DB} = \frac{AE}{EC}
\]

content.title: Using the Side-Splitter Theorem
content.solution:
By the Triangle Proportionality Theorem:
\[
\frac{AD}{DB} = \frac{AE}{EC}
\]
\[
\frac{6}{4} = \frac{9}{EC}
\]

Cross multiply:
\[
6 \cdot EC = 4 \cdot 9 = 36
\]
\[
EC = 6
\]

content.text: Converse of the Triangle Proportionality Theorem

content.title: Converse of Triangle Proportionality
content.content:
If a line divides two sides of a triangle proportionally, then it 
is parallel to the third side.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Proving lines parallel
content.problem:
In $\triangle PQR$, $S$ is on $\overline{PQ}$ and $T$ is on $\overline{PR}$. 
If $PS = 8$, $SQ = 12$, $PT = 6$, and $TR = 9$, is $\overline{ST} \parallel \overline{QR}$?
content.solution:
Check if the sides are divided proportionally:
\[
\frac{PS}{SQ} = \frac{8}{12} = \frac{2}{3}
\]
\[
\frac{PT}{TR} = \frac{6}{9} = \frac{2}{3}
\]

Since $\dfrac{PS}{SQ} = \dfrac{PT}{TR}$, by the Converse of the 
Triangle Proportionality Theorem:

$\overline{ST} \parallel \overline{QR}$

content.text:
The Triangle Proportionality Theorem can be written in several 
equivalent ways. If $\overline{DE} \parallel \overline{BC}$:

content.title: Equivalent Proportions
content.content:
All of the following are equivalent:
\[
\frac{AD}{DB} = \frac{AE}{EC} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad 
\frac{AD}{AB} = \frac{AE}{AC} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad 
\frac{DB}{AB} = \frac{EC}{AC}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Using different forms
content.problem:
In $\triangle ABC$, $\overline{DE} \parallel \overline{BC}$ with 
$AD = 5$, $AB = 15$, and $AC = 21$. Find $AE$ and $EC$.
content.solution:
Using the form $\dfrac{AD}{AB} = \dfrac{AE}{AC}$:
\[
\frac{5}{15} = \frac{AE}{21}
\]
\[
AE = \frac{5 \times 21}{15} = \frac{105}{15} = 7
\]

Therefore, $EC = AC - AE = 21 - 7 = 14$.

*Check*: $\dfrac{AD}{DB} = \dfrac{5}{10} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ and 
$\dfrac{AE}{EC} = \dfrac{7}{14} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

content.text:
Recall the Triangle Midsegment Theorem from earlier in the course: 
A midsegment of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half 
its length.

The midsegment is a special case of the Side-Splitter Theorem. When 
$D$ and $E$ are midpoints:
\[
\frac{AD}{DB} = \frac{AE}{EC} = \frac{1}{1} = 1
\]

This confirms that connecting midpoints creates a segment parallel 
to the base.

content.title: Corollary: Parallel Lines Cut Transversals Proportionally
content.content:
If three or more parallel lines intersect two transversals, then 
they divide the transversals proportionally.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Three parallel lines
content.problem:
Three parallel lines cut two transversals. On one transversal, the 
segments measure 4 cm and 6 cm. On the other transversal, one 
segment measures 10 cm. Find the other segment.
content.solution:
Let the unknown segment be $x$. By the theorem:
\[
\frac{4}{6} = \frac{10}{x}
\]

Cross multiply:
\[
4x = 60
\]
\[
x = 15\text{ cm}
\]

content.text:
Another important proportionality relationship in triangles involves 
angle bisectors.

content.title: Angle Bisector Theorem
content.content:
If a ray bisects an angle of a triangle, then it divides the opposite 
side into segments that are proportional to the other two sides.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Using the Angle Bisector Theorem
content.problem:
In $\triangle ABC$, $\overline{CD}$ bisects $\angle C$. If $AC = 8$, 
$BC = 12$, and $AB = 15$, find $AD$ and $DB$.
content.solution:
By the Angle Bisector Theorem:
\[
\frac{AD}{DB} = \frac{AC}{BC} = \frac{8}{12} = \frac{2}{3}
\]

Let $AD = 2x$ and $DB = 3x$. Then:
\[
AD + DB = AB
\]
\[
2x + 3x = 15
\]
\[
5x = 15
\]
\[
x = 3
\]

Therefore, $AD = 2(3) = 6$ and $DB = 3(3) = 9$.

*Check*: $6 + 9 = 15$ and $\dfrac{6}{9} = \dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{8}{12}$

content.title: Angle bisector with unknown side
content.problem:
In $\triangle PQR$, $\overline{PS}$ bisects $\angle P$, where $S$ is 
on $\overline{QR}$. If $PQ = 10$, $PR = 14$, and $QS = 5$, find $SR$.
content.solution:
By the Angle Bisector Theorem:
\[
\frac{QS}{SR} = \frac{PQ}{PR}
\]
\[
\frac{5}{SR} = \frac{10}{14}
\]
\[
\frac{5}{SR} = \frac{5}{7}
\]
\[
SR = 7
\]

content.text: Proof of the Angle Bisector Theorem

marginNotes.text:
Wisdom has built her house; she has 
hewn her seven pillars.

I will make justice the line, and 
righteousness the plumb line.

Behold, the Lord was standing beside a 
wall built with a plumb line, with a plumb line in his hand.
